If a model uses alternative normalization/residual designs (e.g., reversible layers without multiplicative residual scales), the scalar measure must be mapped to an equivalent gating metric before applying thresholds.
law.discriminating_experiment: Perturb initial residual-scale initializations to produce final s_mean clusters below 0.02 and above 0.03 across many runs; compare exact-match accuracy decay when evaluating on inputs of 2x training length. Success pattern described in the theory_statement supports SAG-RS.
law.falsifies_if: If in the discriminating experiment s_mean <= 0.02 models achieve >=70% of same-length accuracy on 2x-length inputs, or s_mean >= 0.03 models fail to achieve >=70% of same-length accuracy on 2x-length inputs.

law.theory_description: Addition is implemented by repeated localized attention 'sweeps' across digit positions that sequentially propagate carries. Each sweep resolves carries across a contiguous window of W digit positions; the model needs S nonredundant attention-update sweeps to propagate carries across D digits, and correct algorithmic addition occurs only if S >= ceil(D / W). When S < ceil(D / W) the model cannot reliably propagate long carry chains and instead relies on memorized heuristics or statistical shortcuts.

law.theory_statement: Fork: For exact addition of two D-digit base-10 integers tokenized at single digits, if the model provides S attention-update sweeps and attention locality window W (digits) such that S >= ceil(D / W) then carry propagation proceeds algorithmically by localized sweeps (Mechanism A dominates); if S < ceil(D / W) then carry propagation fails systematically and the model relies on memorized or statistical heuristics (Mechanism B dominates). Numeric threshold: S = ceil(D / W). Negative prediction: If S >= ceil(D / W), accuracy on inputs requiring maximal-length carry chains (e.g., 999... + 1) will NOT depend on the frequency of those examples in training and will remain high (>90%) even when such examples occur at frequency <= 1e-5 of training samples. Discriminating experiment: Train decoder-only transformers (vocabulary = digits) on D = 12 digit addition with three enforced attention locality masks producing effective W in {1,2,4}; for each W train model depths L in {8,12,24} and operationalize S as the number of sequential layers producing >5% positional-update norm (measured by per-position embedding change). Make maximal carry examples appear at frequency 1 / 100000 in the training stream. Measure exact-match accuracy on held-out maximal-carry cases and on random holds. If runs with measured S >= ceil(D/W) achieve >90% exact-match on maximal-carry cases despite their rarity and runs with S < ceil(D/W) fail (<40% accuracy) the CSAM statement is supported. Falsifier: Any configuration with measured S >= ceil(D / W) that, after extensive training, fails to reach >90% exact-match on rare maximal-carry cases (or whose performance on those cases scales linearly with example frequency rather than with S) refutes this statement.
law.domain_scope: Decoder-only transformers trained for token-level (single-digit) exact integer addition in base-10, where attention locality can be enforced by sparse-masking; S is measured as the count of layers producing nontrivial cross-position update (operationalized via embedding-change norm). Applies to models without architectural recurrent modules and without multi-digit tokenization.
special_cases: If tokenization groups multiple digits per token (k>1) the effective D and W should be scaled by k; CSAM then applies to token-level positions instead of digit-level.
If a model uses global attention every layer (W = D), then S >= 1 suffices and CSAM reduces to a simple depth threshold.
If residual paths are surgically altered (e.g., removed or scaled), the measured S may change and the ceil(D/W) bound must be recomputed using the new update norm threshold.
law.discriminating_experiment: Train transformer decoders (embedding dim 512) on 12-digit addition with W enforced via sparse masks {1,2,4}. For each mask, train depths L in {8,12,24}. Define a layer as a sweep-step if mean L2 change of position embeddings after that layer >5% of initial embedding norm. Compute S as count of such layers. Insert maximal-carry examples at frequency 1/100k. Evaluate exact-match on maximal-carry held-out set and on random sums. Compare accuracy vs S and vs example frequency. Success pattern: accuracy >90% when S >= ceil(12/W) and <40% otherwise. Alternative patterns falsify CSAM.
law.falsifies_if: Measured S >= ceil(D/W) but model achieves <90% accuracy on rare maximal-carry cases after extensive training, or performance correlates strongly with example frequency and not S.

law.theory_description: This specific theory focuses on how memory representations for spatial navigation in text games should be structured. It proposes that effective navigation in text-based environments requires maintaining an explicit graph-based spatial memory where nodes represent locations/rooms and edges represent traversable connections, with both weighted by multiple factors including visit frequency, traversal reliability, and estimated task importance. The theory posits that language models should augment text observations by querying this spatial graph for: (1) shortest paths to goal locations, (2) unexplored adjacent regions, and (3) recently visited locations to avoid redundant exploration. Performance on navigation-heavy text games scales with the accuracy of the spatial graph representation, the sophistication of graph-based planning queries, and the integration of graph information with language understanding. The key insight is that spatial memory should be structurally distinct from other memory types, organized topologically rather than temporally or semantically, because the graph structure enables efficient planning operations that are computationally intractable with sequence-based or unstructured memory representations.

law.theory_statement: Edge weights in spatial memory graphs should encode multiple factors: (1) traversal reliability (success rate of movement attempts), (2) bidirectional asymmetry (ease of movement A→B may differ from B→A due to one-way passages, elevation changes, or conditional access), (3) traversal cost (time/steps required), and (4) visit frequency for exploration prioritization. Initially uniform or heuristic-based edge weights should be refined through experience, with planning algorithms using these weights to prefer reliable, efficient paths. Games with one-way passages, locked doors, or conditional traversals show 50-80% improvement in navigation success rate and 30-50% reduction in failed movement attempts when using weighted graphs versus unweighted graphs.
law.domain_scope: Applies to text games where navigation connections have variable reliability, conditionality (requiring items/states/actions), directionality, or cost. Most relevant for: (1) puzzle-focused text adventures with locked areas and key-based progression, (2) games with one-way passages or asymmetric movement (climbing up/down, bridges), (3) games with hazardous passages that may fail, and (4) games where different paths have different costs. Applicable across classic parser-based IF, choice-based narrative games with spatial components, and text RPGs with conditional movement.
special_cases: In games with perfectly reliable bidirectional movement where all edges are always traversable and symmetric, weighted graphs provide minimal advantage (<10%) over unweighted graphs, though may still help with exploration prioritization.

If traversal conditions change dynamically and unpredictably during gameplay (e.g., random door locking), weight updates must be sufficiently frequent or the graph may become misleading and hurt performance compared to treating all weights as uncertain.
Very simple games without conditional movement, one-way passages, or movement failures may not benefit from reliability-based weighting, though visit frequency weighting may still help exploration.
In games with extremely complex conditional movement (requiring many items/states), binary reachability may be more useful than continuous weights until conditions are fully understood.
Edge weight learning requires sufficient exploration experience; early in gameplay, learned weights may be unreliable and heuristic or uniform weights may perform better.

law.theory_description: This theory proposes that effective memory augmentation for language model agents in text games requires dynamic adjustment of memory scope—the temporal and semantic range of experiences considered relevant—based on task demands, agent competency, and environmental uncertainty. The theory integrates principles from reinforcement learning, cognitive science, and language modeling to explain how memory systems should adapt their retrieval strategies across different phases of learning and different types of game states. Specifically, it posits that: (1) retrieval bandwidth (number of memories accessed) should scale with epistemic uncertainty, (2) memory organization should progressively specialize from general-purpose to task-specific structures as experience accumulates, (3) retrieval scope should inversely correlate with agent competence in specific situations, and (4) temporal lag between experience and consolidation improves memory quality by enabling context accumulation. These principles work together to create an adaptive curriculum-like effect where memory becomes increasingly efficient and specialized as agents gain experience.

law.theory_statement: As language model agents accumulate experience in text game environments over extended training, effective memory organization evolves from general-purpose, unstructured storage toward specialized, functionally-organized subsystems. Early in training (few hundred episodes), agents benefit from flexible memory that stores diverse experiences without strong organizational structure. After substantial experience (thousands of episodes in complex games), performance is enhanced by memory subsystems specialized for different task types: navigation/spatial memory (room layouts, connections), inventory/object memory (item properties, combinations), dialogue memory (NPC interactions, conversation history), and puzzle/causal memory (action-outcome patterns). The degree of benefit from specialization correlates with the heterogeneity of task types in the game environment.
law.domain_scope: Applies to memory-augmented language model agents learning complex text games over extended training periods (1000+ episodes or equivalent) where: (1) the game environment contains multiple distinct types of challenges (navigation, inventory puzzles, dialogue, combat, etc.), (2) the agent has sufficient memory capacity to support specialized subsystems, (3) training is long enough for organizational structure to emerge or be learned, and (4) the game is complex enough that task heterogeneity makes specialization beneficial. Most relevant for large-scale parser-based interactive fiction (Zork, Anchorhead) or complex procedurally generated environments (TextWorld with multiple task types).
special_cases: In single-task or homogeneous environments (e.g., games that are purely navigation or purely dialogue), specialization provides minimal benefit over general memory.
If the agent requires frequent transfer to new game variants or genres, excessive specialization may harm generalization and adaptability.
Very short training regimes (<500 episodes) may not provide sufficient experience for specialization to emerge or show benefits.
For small-scale games with limited state spaces, the overhead of maintaining specialized memory subsystems may outweigh benefits.
If using very large pre-trained language models with strong general capabilities, the marginal benefit of specialized memory organization may be reduced.

law.theory_description: Arithmetic is implemented by attention heads that route carry information sparsely to a small set of downstream positions. When the effective number of attended positions per head (Neff = exp(H_attention)) is <= 3, carry routing circuits dominate and produce algorithmic carry propagation; when Neff >= 6, attention is diffuse and memorized mapping dominates.

law.statement_name: Attention sparsity fork for carry routing
law.theory_statement: Fork: For transformer models trained from scratch on base-10 integer addition, if the per-head effective attended positions Neff (exp of attention entropy, averaged over heads and informative token positions) is <= 3 then Mechanism A dominates: heads implement sparse routing enabling correct carry propagation across arbitrary distances; if Neff >= 6 then Mechanism B dominates: attention is diffuse, preventing reliable carry routing and causing failures on cross-boundary carries. Numeric thresholds: Neff <= 3 vs Neff >= 6. Negative prediction: Models with Neff <= 3 will NOT show systematic cross-token carry failures (exact-match on carry cases >=90%); models with Neff >= 6 will NOT exceed 40% exact-match on cross-token carry cases under identical training. Discriminating experiment: Train a population of identical transformers (L=16, width=1024) on two-operand additions up to 8 digits. After training, compute per-head Neff for positions near potential carry sites. Then (a) prune/zero out the top-K attention weights in heads to force Neff to drop below 3 and measure whether models that previously failed now generalize on cross-boundary carry tests; (b) conversely, apply random smoothing (softmax temperature increase) to raise Neff above 6 in models that previously succeeded and measure whether success degrades. If dropping Neff to <=3 recovers carry generalization and raising it above 6 destroys generalization consistently, CSAR is supported. Falsifier: If manipulating Neff as above does not change cross-boundary carry generalization (e.g., increasing Neff above 6 while preserving weights does not reduce carry success, or decreasing Neff to <=3 does not improve carry success), CSAR is falsified.
law.domain_scope: Transformer models trained from scratch on base-10 integer addition, measuring attention distributions from the softmax of scaled dot-product attention. Neff is computed over token positions excluding padding and averaged over heads and positions that are carry-relevant (digit positions). Training uses uniformly sampled digit combinations; no chain-of-thought supervision.
special_cases: If a model intentionally uses explicit positional carry registers (extra tokens reserved for carry), the relation to Neff changes; CSAR applies to models that implement carry via standard attention patterns between digit tokens.
In architectures with hard sparse attention mechanisms (top-k attention), Neff is discrete and thresholds must be interpreted accordingly (e.g., top-3 corresponds to Neff <=3).
law.discriminating_experiment: Prune top attention weights to force Neff <=3 in failing models and apply softmax temperature to increase Neff >=6 in succeeding models; measure effect on cross-boundary carry exact-match accuracy. Observed symmetric causal changes support CSAR.
law.falsifies_if: If manipulating Neff as described does not change cross-boundary carry performance (no consistent improvement when Neff <=3 or no consistent degradation when Neff >=6), CSAR is falsified.

law.theory_description: This theory proposes that effective memory augmentation for language model agents in text games requires dynamic adjustment of memory scope—the temporal and semantic range of experiences considered relevant—based on task demands, agent competency, and environmental uncertainty. The theory integrates principles from reinforcement learning, cognitive science, and language modeling to explain how memory systems should adapt their retrieval strategies across different phases of learning and different types of game states. Specifically, it posits that: (1) retrieval bandwidth (number of memories accessed) should scale with epistemic uncertainty, (2) memory organization should progressively specialize from general-purpose to task-specific structures as experience accumulates, (3) retrieval scope should inversely correlate with agent competence in specific situations, and (4) temporal lag between experience and consolidation improves memory quality by enabling context accumulation. These principles work together to create an adaptive curriculum-like effect where memory becomes increasingly efficient and specialized as agents gain experience.

law.theory_statement: Effective memory augmentation in text game agents benefits from temporal lag between initial experience and consolidation into long-term memory. Rather than immediately encoding all experiences into permanent memory, maintaining experiences in a short-term buffer (10-100 steps) before selective consolidation leads to higher-quality long-term memories. During the lag period: (1) delayed outcomes and rewards become available, enabling better credit assignment, (2) experiences accumulate temporal context from subsequent events, (3) redundant or low-value experiences can be filtered out, and (4) related experiences can be compressed or aggregated. The optimal lag duration correlates with the game's temporal credit assignment window—games with longer action-outcome delays benefit from longer consolidation lags.
law.domain_scope: Applies to text game agents with dual-buffer or multi-stage memory systems (short-term/working memory and long-term memory) in environments where: (1) actions have delayed consequences (typically >5 steps between action and outcome), (2) episodes are long enough for lag to matter (>100 steps), (3) rewards are sparse or delayed, (4) memory capacity is limited requiring selective consolidation, and (5) the agent can identify which experiences warrant long-term storage. Most relevant for puzzle-heavy games (Zork, Anchorhead), games with complex causal chains, and procedurally generated environments with sparse rewards (TextWorld).
special_cases: In games with immediate feedback (every action immediately rewarded/penalized), consolidation lag provides minimal benefit and may unnecessarily delay learning.
If short-term memory capacity is severely limited (<10 experiences), immediate consolidation may be necessary despite quality trade-offs.
In very long episodes (>1000 steps), fixed-duration lag may be insufficient and adaptive lag based on outcome availability may be needed.
When using large replay buffers that already provide temporal context, additional consolidation lag may be redundant.
If the agent has perfect or near-perfect memory capacity, the need for selective consolidation and associated lag is reduced.

It proposes that effective navigation in text-based environments requires maintaining an explicit graph-based spatial memory where nodes represent locations/rooms and edges represent traversable connections, with both weighted by multiple factors including visit frequency, traversal reliability, and estimated task importance. The theory posits that language models should augment text observations by querying this spatial graph for: (1) shortest paths to goal locations, (2) unexplored adjacent regions, and (3) recently visited locations to avoid redundant exploration. Performance on navigation-heavy text games scales with the accuracy of the spatial graph representation, the sophistication of graph-based planning queries, and the integration of graph information with language understanding. The key insight is that spatial memory should be structurally distinct from other memory types, organized topologically rather than temporally or semantically, because the graph structure enables efficient planning operations that are computationally intractable with sequence-based or unstructured memory representations.

law.theory_statement: For text games with spatial navigation components (where >30% of actions involve movement between discrete locations), memory systems that maintain explicit graph-structured spatial representations outperform flat or sequence-based memory by 40-70% on navigation efficiency metrics including steps to reach goals, redundant location visits, and planning time. The performance gap increases systematically with world size (number of distinct locations) and graph connectivity complexity (average edges per node), with the advantage becoming most pronounced in worlds with >50 locations and connectivity >3 edges/node.
law.domain_scope: Applies to text-based interactive games and environments with discrete location-based navigation where: (1) rooms/areas are connected by explicit directional movements (north, south, enter door, etc.), (2) the spatial structure is learnable and relatively stable, and (3) navigation represents a substantial component of the task. Most applicable to classic text adventures (Zork, Adventureland), parser-based interactive fiction, text-based RPGs with exploration, and MUD-style environments. Less applicable to dialogue-heavy games, puzzle games without spatial components, or games with abstract state spaces that don't correspond to physical locations.
special_cases: In games with very simple linear geography (corridor-like structures, <10 locations in a chain), graph structure provides minimal advantage (<20%) as sequence memory can adequately represent the space.

In games with dynamic/changing geography where location connections change based on game state or time, graph maintenance overhead and outdated information may reduce advantages to 20-30% unless update mechanisms are sophisticated.
For games where navigation is trivial (<10% of challenge, or <5 locations total), investment in sophisticated spatial memory infrastructure may not be worthwhile relative to simpler approaches.
In procedurally generated environments where spatial structure changes between episodes, transfer learning of spatial representations may be limited unless abstract topological features are learned.

When combined with very large language models (>100B parameters) that may implicitly encode spatial reasoning, the explicit graph advantage may be reduced but still present (20-40% improvement) due to computational efficiency of explicit structure.

law.theory_description: This theory proposes that effective memory augmentation for language model agents in text games requires dynamic adjustment of memory scope—the temporal and semantic range of experiences considered relevant—based on task demands, agent competency, and environmental uncertainty. The theory integrates principles from reinforcement learning, cognitive science, and language modeling to explain how memory systems should adapt their retrieval strategies across different phases of learning and different types of game states. Specifically, it posits that: (1) retrieval bandwidth (number of memories accessed) should scale with epistemic uncertainty, (2) memory organization should progressively specialize from general-purpose to task-specific structures as experience accumulates, (3) retrieval scope should inversely correlate with agent competence in specific situations, and (4) temporal lag between experience and consolidation improves memory quality by enabling context accumulation. These principles work together to create an adaptive curriculum-like effect where memory becomes increasingly efficient and specialized as agents gain experience.

law.theory_statement: Memory retrieval scope (both temporal range and semantic diversity) should vary inversely with agent competence in the current situation. When agents have high competence—indicated by high value function confidence, low policy entropy, previous success in similar states, or low surprise—they should use narrow-scope retrieval (recent experiences, high similarity threshold, small temporal window). When agents have low competence—indicated by high value uncertainty, high policy entropy, novel states, or high surprise—they should use broad-scope retrieval (temporally distant experiences, diverse semantic matches, large temporal window). This inverse coupling enables efficient memory use: computational resources for memory search are allocated where they're most needed (uncertain situations), while conserving resources in familiar situations.
law.domain_scope: Applies to memory-augmented text game agents where: (1) the agent maintains estimates of competence, confidence, or uncertainty (value function uncertainty, policy entropy, state novelty scores, or prediction error), (2) retrieval scope can be adjusted via parameters (temporal window size, similarity threshold, number of retrievals), (3) the game environment has heterogeneous difficulty with regions where agent competence varies substantially, (4) memory retrieval has non-trivial computational cost, and (5) the agent's memory contains both relevant and potentially distracting experiences. Most applicable in large, complex games (Zork, commercial IF) where agents encounter mixture of familiar and novel states.
special_cases: In uniformly difficult games where agent competence remains consistently low throughout, maintaining broad retrieval scope may be necessary rather than adaptive.
If competence estimates are systematically miscalibrated (overconfident or underconfident), the coupling may degrade performance rather than improve it.
In time-critical real-time games, computational costs may override competence-based scope adjustment.
When memory quality varies substantially (some periods have high-quality experiences, others have noise), scope adjustment may need to account for memory quality in addition to competence.
For very large language models with strong in-context learning, explicit memory and scope adjustment may be less critical.

law.theory_description: This specific theory focuses on how memory representations for spatial navigation in text games should be structured. It proposes that effective navigation in text-based environments requires maintaining an explicit graph-based spatial memory where nodes represent locations/rooms and edges represent traversable connections, with both weighted by multiple factors including visit frequency, traversal reliability, and estimated task importance. The theory posits that language models should augment text observations by querying this spatial graph for: (1) shortest paths to goal locations, (2) unexplored adjacent regions, and (3) recently visited locations to avoid redundant exploration. Performance on navigation-heavy text games scales with the accuracy of the spatial graph representation, the sophistication of graph-based planning queries, and the integration of graph information with language understanding. The key insight is that spatial memory should be structurally distinct from other memory types, organized topologically rather than temporally or semantically, because the graph structure enables efficient planning operations that are computationally intractable with sequence-based or unstructured memory representations.

law.theory_statement: Spatial graphs should be constructed incrementally during exploration with probabilistic confidence scores on both nodes (location existence, properties, and descriptions) and edges (connection existence, properties, and weights). Early exploration with low confidence (<0.6) should trigger verification behaviors (revisiting to confirm connections, testing edges). Planning should balance multiple objectives: shortest paths, path confidence, and exploration value. Specifically, high-confidence longer paths may be preferred over low-confidence shorter paths when the risk-adjusted expected cost favors reliability. This confidence-aware planning reduces navigation failures by 30-50% and reduces wasted actions from incorrect spatial assumptions by 40-60% compared to confidence-naive approaches that assume all parsed information is correct.
law.domain_scope: Applies to text games where spatial information must be learned through exploration and may be ambiguous, uncertain, or incorrect from text descriptions alone. Most relevant for: (1) complex worlds where descriptions are terse, ambiguous, or use varied language, (2) games where spatial structure must be inferred from narrative descriptions rather than explicit maps, (3) environments where the parser or language understanding may produce errors, and (4) games with partially observable states where spatial information is revealed gradually. Applicable to parser-based IF with complex descriptions, narrative-heavy games where space is described rather than diagrammed, and games where spatial reasoning is required but not explicitly supported.
special_cases: In games with complete, unambiguous, explicit spatial descriptions (e.g., 'Exits: north, south, east') where parsing is highly reliable, confidence tracking adds unnecessary computational overhead (5-10%) with minimal benefit (<5% performance gain).
If exploration is unconstrained and inexpensive (no hazards, unlimited time/steps), agents may prefer exploration over confidence-aware planning since verification costs are low.
Games with procedurally generated or randomized layouts may require different confidence update mechanisms, as patterns learned in one episode may not transfer.
In early gameplay when all confidence values are low, confidence-based planning may produce conservative behavior that delays progress; bootstrapping mechanisms may be needed.
Very large language models with strong spatial reasoning may produce high-confidence spatial inferences that are actually incorrect (overconfidence), requiring calibration.
Confidence score maintenance adds memory and computational overhead (typically 10-20% increase), which may not be worthwhile in resource-constrained settings for simple games.

law.theory_description: This specific theory focuses on how memory representations for spatial navigation in text games should be structured. It proposes that effective navigation in text-based environments requires maintaining an explicit graph-based spatial memory where nodes represent locations/rooms and edges represent traversable connections, with both weighted by multiple factors including visit frequency, traversal reliability, and estimated task importance. The theory posits that language models should augment text observations by querying this spatial graph for: (1) shortest paths to goal locations, (2) unexplored adjacent regions, and (3) recently visited locations to avoid redundant exploration. Performance on navigation-heavy text games scales with the accuracy of the spatial graph representation, the sophistication of graph-based planning queries, and the integration of graph information with language understanding. The key insight is that spatial memory should be structurally distinct from other memory types, organized topologically rather than temporally or semantically, because the graph structure enables efficient planning operations that are computationally intractable with sequence-based or unstructured memory representations.

law.theory_statement: Effective spatial graph memory systems must support multiple query types that provide complementary information to the language model agent: (1) goal-directed queries (shortest/best paths to specific locations), (2) exploration queries (identifying unexplored or under-visited regions), (3) context queries (recently visited locations and spatial context), and (4) reachability queries (what locations are accessible given current state/items). These queries should be integrated into the agent's decision-making process by augmenting text observations with structured spatial information. Agents using all four query types outperform those using only goal-directed queries by 30-50% on overall task completion, with exploration queries contributing 15-25% improvement, context queries 10-15%, and reachability queries 20-30% in games with conditional access.
law.domain_scope: Applies to text games where spatial navigation is integrated with other task objectives (finding items, solving puzzles, completing quests) rather than pure navigation tasks. Most relevant for: (1) adventure games with multiple objectives distributed across space, (2) RPGs where spatial exploration reveals new quests/items, (3) puzzle games where spatial structure is part of the puzzle, and (4) games requiring backtracking and non-linear progression. Applies across most text game genres except purely linear narratives or pure puzzle games without spatial components.
special_cases: In pure navigation tasks with a single goal, only goal-directed queries are necessary, and other query types add minimal value (<5%).
In fully linear games, exploration queries provide no value as there are no choices to make.
In games with complete knowledge of spatial structure from the start, exploration queries are unnecessary.
Context queries are most valuable in games with limited text history or context windows, less valuable with unlimited history.
Reachability queries are only valuable in games with conditional access (locked doors, required items); in fully open worlds they provide minimal information.
Query overhead (computation time) must be balanced against benefits; in real-time games, expensive queries may not be feasible.

law.theory_description: This specific theory addresses how memory should encode and utilize causal relationships between actions and their outcomes in text games. It proposes that beyond storing state-action-reward tuples, effective memory systems must explicitly represent causal chains: sequences of actions that led to particular outcomes, including intervening states and temporal delays. The theory posits that these causal memories should be indexed by outcome type (inventory acquisition, puzzle solution, location access, NPC state changes) and retrievable when facing similar outcome requirements. When planning, agents should query causal memory for 'how was outcome O achieved previously?' and adapt those action sequences to the current context through variable substitution and contextual adjustment. Performance on puzzle-solving and goal-oriented tasks correlates with the richness of causal chain representations, the quality of causal reasoning during retrieval, and the effectiveness of adaptation mechanisms.

law.theory_statement: Causal memories should be indexed and retrievable by outcome type and outcome properties (e.g., 'acquired item of type X', 'solved puzzle requiring tool use', 'defeated enemy with vulnerability Y', 'accessed location Z') rather than by initial state features alone. When facing a new goal requiring outcome O, retrieving past causal chains that achieved outcomes similar to O (measured by outcome feature overlap or outcome category membership) enables substantially higher success rates (estimated 40-70% improvement) through analogical transfer and adaptation compared to retrieval based solely on initial state similarity or recency. The effectiveness of outcome-indexed retrieval increases with: (1) the richness of outcome type taxonomy, (2) the diversity of initial states leading to similar outcomes, and (3) the agent's ability to recognize outcome similarity.
law.domain_scope: Applies to goal-directed text game tasks where: (a) agents must achieve specific, identifiable outcomes (acquire items, solve puzzles, defeat enemies, access locations, change NPC states), (b) similar outcome types recur with variations across the game, (c) multiple different initial state configurations can lead to similar outcomes, and (d) outcomes can be classified into meaningful categories or described by feature vectors. Most relevant for games with structured goal taxonomies, diverse puzzle instances of similar types, and explicit goal specifications.
special_cases: In purely exploratory gameplay without specific goals or success criteria, outcome-indexed retrieval is not applicable as there are no well-defined outcomes to index by.
If outcomes are entirely unique with no similarity structure (each outcome is completely novel), outcome-based indexing provides no advantage as no retrieved memories will match.

When initial state similarity is highly predictive of outcome similarity (deterministic mapping from state type to outcome type), state-based retrieval may perform comparably or better due to simpler implementation.

If the outcome taxonomy is poorly designed (too coarse or too fine-grained), retrieval effectiveness degrades: overly coarse categories retrieve irrelevant memories, overly fine categories fail to find matches.
In games where outcomes are implicit or difficult to extract from text, outcome-indexed retrieval requires sophisticated outcome detection, otherwise indexing will be noisy.

It proposes that beyond storing state-action-reward tuples, effective memory systems must explicitly represent causal chains: sequences of actions that led to particular outcomes, including intervening states and temporal delays. The theory posits that these causal memories should be indexed by outcome type (inventory acquisition, puzzle solution, location access, NPC state changes) and retrievable when facing similar outcome requirements. When planning, agents should query causal memory for 'how was outcome O achieved previously?' and adapt those action sequences to the current context through variable substitution and contextual adjustment. Performance on puzzle-solving and goal-oriented tasks correlates with the richness of causal chain representations, the quality of causal reasoning during retrieval, and the effectiveness of adaptation mechanisms.

law.theory_statement: Stored causal chains should be abstracted from specific entities and actions to general patterns at multiple levels of granularity (e.g., 'obtain tool → use tool on obstacle → access new area' rather than 'get rusty key → unlock old door → enter tower', or more abstractly 'obtain enabling resource → apply resource to barrier → access restricted region'). This hierarchical abstraction enables transfer across superficially different but structurally similar puzzles. Performance on novel puzzle variants improves substantially (estimated 50-80% increase in success rate) with appropriately abstracted causal memory compared to concrete instance storage. The optimal abstraction level balances generality (enabling broad transfer) with specificity (preserving causally-relevant details), and may vary by puzzle domain. Multiple abstraction levels should be maintained simultaneously to enable both fine-grained and coarse-grained matching.
law.domain_scope: Applies to text games with: (a) recurring puzzle structures across different thematic contexts (e.g., various lock-and-key puzzles, trading chains, combat sequences with different entities), (b) compositional action and object spaces where entities can be categorized by properties and affordances, (c) multiple similar-structured but differently-themed challenges, and (d) sufficient puzzle diversity to benefit from generalization (at least 5-10 puzzle instances per structural type). Most relevant for games with systematic design patterns, entity type hierarchies, and procedural puzzle generation.
special_cases: In games where specific entity properties are critical to solutions (e.g., a puzzle requiring specifically 'cold' items, not just any tool), over-abstraction that loses these details will fail. The abstraction must preserve causally-relevant properties.
If puzzles are truly unique without structural similarities (each puzzle uses completely novel mechanics), abstraction provides no transfer benefit as no structural patterns recur.
Very simple games with limited puzzle diversity may not have sufficient instances per pattern to benefit from abstraction, as the overhead of learning abstractions exceeds benefits.
When entity types are ambiguous or not clearly defined in the game world, automatic abstraction may create incorrect generalizations that group causally distinct cases.
In games with very large type hierarchies, determining the appropriate abstraction level becomes complex and may require meta-learning or explicit specification.
Abstractions that are too general may match too many irrelevant cases, while abstractions that are too specific may match too few cases, both degrading retrieval precision.

law.theory_statement: Memory systems that explicitly encode action sequences as causal chains linking initial states through intermediate states to eventual outcomes (e.g., 'obtained key → unlocked door → reached treasure room') enable substantially better performance (estimated 30-60% improvement in task completion time and success rate) on similar future tasks compared to storing individual state-action pairs or flat episodic traces. The performance benefit increases monotonically with: (1) outcome delay (number of steps between initiating action and consequence), (2) the frequency of structurally similar puzzle patterns in the game, and (3) the complexity of state dependencies in the causal chain.
law.domain_scope: Applies to text-based interactive fiction and text adventure games with puzzle-solving elements where: (a) actions have delayed multi-step consequences (>3 steps between action and reward), (b) similar puzzle patterns recur with variations (at least 2-3 instances of structurally similar puzzles), (c) intermediate states are partially observable or must be inferred, and (d) the action space is discrete and compositional. Most effective in games with inventory-based puzzles, lock-and-key mechanics, crafting systems, quest chains, and NPC interaction sequences where causal dependencies span multiple game states.
special_cases: In games with only immediate consequences (reward within 1-2 steps), causal chain encoding provides minimal advantage (<5% improvement) over simple state-action storage, as temporal credit assignment is trivial.

If puzzles are entirely unique without recurring structural patterns (zero-shot puzzle solving), causal chain retrieval provides no transfer benefit, though encoding may still aid credit assignment within a single puzzle instance.

Very long causal chains (>50 steps) face two challenges: (a) difficulty in accurate learning due to long-term credit assignment, and (b) reduced reusability as longer chains tend to be more context-specific.
In stochastic environments where action outcomes are probabilistic, causal chains must be annotated with success conditions and failure modes, otherwise retrieval may suggest unreliable strategies.
When multiple alternative causal paths lead to the same outcome, the memory system must either store multiple chains or abstract over them, otherwise coverage is incomplete.

law.theory_statement: Causal memory systems must be integrated with other memory types (spatial/location memory, dialogue/NPC state memory, inventory memory, game rule knowledge) through explicit cross-references and consistency constraints. When retrieving causal chains, the system should verify that prerequisite conditions (spatial accessibility, inventory requirements, NPC preconditions) are satisfied in the current context before applying the chain. Performance degrades by 30-50% if causal chains are applied without contextual verification, leading to invalid action sequences. The integration should be bidirectional: causal chains inform what other memory to retrieve, and other memory types constrain which causal chains are applicable.
law.domain_scope: Applies to complex text games with: (a) multiple interacting game systems (spatial navigation, inventory, NPC dialogue, puzzle-solving), (b) prerequisite conditions for action applicability (location constraints, inventory requirements, NPC state dependencies), (c) rich contextual state that affects action outcomes, and (d) need for multi-step reasoning across different knowledge types. Most relevant for large-scale interactive fiction, RPG-style text games, and complex adventure games.
special_cases: In simple single-system games (e.g., pure puzzle games without spatial navigation or inventory), integration with other memory types is unnecessary as causal chains are self-contained.
If prerequisite conditions are always explicitly stated in the current observation, verification may be redundant as invalid actions will be obviously inapplicable.
When causal chains are only stored at high abstraction levels, they may not contain sufficient detail for fine-grained consistency checking with other memory systems.
In games with weak coupling between systems (changes in one system rarely affect others), integration overhead may exceed benefits.
